title: Generics
objectives: Understand generics with a real-life analogy
Write generic classes and methods with type safety and no casting
Understand type erasure and its consequences

sections.markdown:
**Imagine buying a labeled cardboard box at IKEA.**

- An **unlabeled box** (`ArrayList`, no type parameter) can hold anything — books, shoes, dishes, someone's cat. But when you unpack, you're guessing what's inside. If you assume 'books' but it's dishes, they break (`ClassCastException`).
- A **labeled box** (`ArrayList<Book>`) has 'BOOKS' printed on it. The store won't sell it to you filled with dishes. When you unpack, you *know* everything is a Book — no casting, no guessing.

**Generics are the label.** They let the compiler enforce, at compile time, that a `List<Book>` only contains Books — turning a runtime crash into a compile error. That's why generics exist: safer code, cleaner code, no casts.

**Generics** let you parameterize types over other types — `List<String>`, `Map<Long, User>`, `Optional<Order>`. They give you compile-time type safety and remove explicit casts. Introduced in Java 5, they're used everywhere in the standard library.
## The four ways generics show up

## Bounded wildcards — the PECS rule

**PECS** = **P**roducer **E**xtends, **C**onsumer **S**uper. It's the single most important thing to memorize about generics.

**PECS explained plainly:** if the collection is *giving you* values, use `extends` (you're consuming what it produces). If the collection is *receiving* your values, use `super` (you're feeding into its consumer). Interviewers love this — 90% of Java engineers can't explain PECS clearly on the spot.

## Type erasure — the Big Rule with Big Consequences

At compile time, generics enforce types. At runtime, they're *erased* — the JVM sees only raw types. This has real consequences:
## A complete sample program — generic Cache with all the pieces
## Common beginner mistakes

**Mistake 1: Using raw types.**

```java
List list = new ArrayList();          // raw — no type checking!
list.add("a");
list.add(42);
String s = (String) list.get(1);      // ClassCastException at runtime
```

Always parameterize: `List<String> list = new ArrayList<>();`. Raw types are a Java 1.4 backwards-compatibility relic. Modern IDEs warn on them.

**Mistake 2: Getting PECS backwards.**

If a method returns values, use `extends`. If it accepts values, use `super`. Mixing them up produces baffling 'can't add to this list' compile errors. Rule of thumb: **P**roducer **E**xtends, **C**onsumer **S**uper — memorize it.

**Mistake 3: Trying to `new T()`.**

Due to erasure, `T` doesn't exist at runtime. Workaround: pass `Class<T>` and use reflection: `T t = cls.getDeclaredConstructor().newInstance()`. Or accept a `Supplier<T>` factory instead.
## Best practices
**1. Always parameterize collections.** `List<String>`, not raw `List`. The compiler is your friend.
**2. Use diamond operator `<>` (Java 7+):** `List<Map<String, Integer>> x = new ArrayList<>();` — no need to repeat the type parameters on the right side.
**3. Prefer `? extends` in reader-only APIs**, `? super` in writer-only APIs, and specific `T` when you both read and write.
**4. Suppress `@SuppressWarnings("unchecked")` sparingly and only after checking the cast is genuinely safe.** Ideally with a comment explaining why.
## Practice programs

**Practice 1 (Easy) — generic `max(List<T extends Comparable<T>>)`.** Return the biggest element. Bounded to Comparables.

**Why:** Combines generic method + bounded type.

**Practice 2 (Medium) — generic `Stack<T>`.** With push, pop, peek. Backed by an ArrayList.

**Why:** Classic exercise — teaches you generic class internals.

**Practice 3 (Hard) — `copy(List<? extends T> src, List<? super T> dst)`.** Prove PECS is required — try to write it without wildcards and see the errors.

**Why:** The single most common bounded-wildcards exercise.

**Practice 4 (Expert) — heterogeneous container.** A `TypeSafeMap` where `put(Class<T> key, T value)` and `get(Class<T> key)` return the right type. Uses Class<T> as a key to survive erasure.

**Why:** A famous Effective Java pattern; understanding it shows you understand erasure deeply.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What problem do generics solve, and what did we do before them?**

**A:** Before generics (Java 1.4 and earlier), collections held `Object`. When you got a value out, you had to cast it: `String s = (String) list.get(0);` — with no compile-time guarantee it was a String. If it wasn't, you got a `ClassCastException` at runtime, often far from the original mistake. **Generics** (Java 5) let you parameterize types: `List<String>` guarantees at compile time that only Strings go in and come out. No casts, no runtime type errors from mixed-up collections. The compiler enforces safety. That's the single biggest win: turn runtime crashes into compile errors.

**Q2: Explain PECS with an example.**

**A:** **PECS** = **P**roducer **E**xtends, **C**onsumer **S**uper. Use `? extends T` when the parameter is a *producer* — you'll only read T out of it. Use `? super T` when it's a *consumer* — you'll only put T into it. **Example:** `copy(List<? extends Number> src, List<? super Number> dst)`. `src` is the producer (you read Numbers out of it — could be `List<Integer>` or `List<Double>`). `dst` is the consumer (you add Numbers into it — could be `List<Number>` or `List<Object>`). If you tried to make both `List<Number>`, `copy(List<Integer>, List<Object>)` would fail to compile — even though logically it's fine. PECS lets the compiler accept flexible types.

**Q3: What is type erasure, and what are its consequences?**

**A:** At runtime, all generic type information is erased — the JVM sees `List` regardless of whether you wrote `List<String>` or `List<Integer>`. Consequences: (1) You can't do `if (x instanceof List<String>)` — no runtime type info. (2) You can't do `new T()` — the compiler doesn't know what T is at that point. Workaround: pass `Class<T>` explicitly. (3) You can't overload methods that differ only in generic parameters — `print(List<String>)` and `print(List<Integer>)` collide because both erase to `print(List)`. (4) You can't create `T[]` arrays directly. Erasure exists for **backwards compatibility** — pre-5 code could still compile against Java 5+ collections. It's the price we paid for generics in Java. C# reified generics (kept at runtime) but at the cost of a bigger break.

**Q4: Why are raw types discouraged?**

**A:** Raw types (`List` instead of `List<String>`) exist only for backwards compatibility with pre-Java 5 code. They opt out of type checking entirely — the compiler accepts anything you `add()` and gives you `Object` on `get()`, forcing you to cast (and risk `ClassCastException`). Using raw types in new code defeats the whole purpose of generics: safety. Modern IDEs (IntelliJ, Eclipse) flag raw types as warnings. The one place raw types still appear naturally is in reflection APIs, and even there, `Class<?>` is preferred over raw `Class`.

**Q5: Can you create a generic array in Java? Why or why not?**

**A:** No — you can't write `new T[10]` or `new List<String>[10]`. Arrays are **reified** (they know their component type at runtime) but generics are **erased** (they don't). If Java allowed `T[]`, you could put anything in and violate type safety silently. Concrete example: if `List<String>[] a = new List<String>[10]` were allowed, someone could assign `a[0] = new ArrayList<Integer>()` via Object[] indirection — the compiler couldn't stop it. Workaround: use `(T[]) new Object[10]` with `@SuppressWarnings("unchecked")` and pinky-swear it's safe. Or, better, use `ArrayList<T>` instead of a generic array.

**Q6: What's the difference between `List<Object>`, `List<?>`, and raw `List`?**

**A:** **`List<Object>`** is a list that specifically holds Objects. You can add anything (all objects are Objects). But you *cannot* assign a `List<String>` to it — because if you could, someone could add an Integer through the `List<Object>` reference, breaking the original `List<String>`'s guarantee. **`List<?>`** (unbounded wildcard) is 'a list of some unknown type.' You can read Objects from it, but you can't add anything (except null) — because the compiler doesn't know what type it should be. You *can* assign a `List<String>` or `List<Integer>` to it — useful when the operation is type-agnostic. **`List`** (raw) opts out of generics entirely — anything goes, no type checking. Use `List<?>` when you don't care about the element type; never use raw.

**Q7: What is a bounded type parameter?**

**A:** A type parameter with a restriction: `<T extends Number>` means 'T can only be Number or a subclass of Number.' Useful when your code needs to call methods on T. Example: `<T extends Comparable<T>> T max(List<T> list)` — T must be Comparable so you can call `.compareTo`. Multiple bounds are allowed with `&`: `<T extends Number & Comparable<T>>`. Only the first bound can be a class; the rest must be interfaces. Bounded parameters combine the flexibility of generics with the safety of type checks — the compiler knows T has the bounded methods available.

## Real-world examples

- **The entire Java Collections framework** is generic: `List<T>`, `Map<K, V>`, `Set<T>` — safer than pre-5 collections that held raw `Object`.
- **Spring's `RestTemplate` and `WebClient`** use generics for typed response bodies: `List<User> users = restTemplate.exchange(url, GET, null, new ParameterizedTypeReference<List<User>>(){}).getBody();`.
- **`Optional<T>`, `Stream<T>`, `Future<T>`, `CompletableFuture<T>`** — every modern Java API is generic.
- **Retrofit, Jackson, Gson** all use generic method signatures with `TypeToken<T>` or `TypeReference<T>` to work around erasure for JSON deserialization.

## Advanced notes

- **Recursive type bounds** — `class Enum<E extends Enum<E>>`. Powerful but mind-bending. Used in `Comparable<T>`, `Enum<E>`.
- **Reifiable vs non-reifiable types** — an int[] is reifiable (JVM knows it's int); a `List<String>` is non-reifiable (JVM only knows it's a List). Determines what instanceof checks work.
- **`TypeToken` (Gson) and `ParameterizedTypeReference` (Spring)** — the standard trick to preserve type info across erasure using anonymous subclasses.
- **Kotlin reified type parameters** — Kotlin can preserve generic info via `inline fun <reified T> ...`. Java has no equivalent.
- **Bridge methods** — the compiler sometimes synthesizes 'bridge' methods to preserve polymorphism across erasure. Rarely visible unless you inspect bytecode.
- **Wildcards vs generic methods** — often either works. Prefer generic methods when the type parameter appears in multiple places; wildcards for single-use flexibility.

flashcards.back: Producer Extends, Consumer Super. `? extends T` when you read T out. `? super T` when you put T in.
At runtime, the JVM sees raw types — `List<String>` and `List<Integer>` are both just `List`. Type info is only compile-time.
They opt out of type checking; you're back to Java 1.4 casting-and-praying.

flashcards.front: Can you `new T()` inside a generic class?
flashcards.back: No — T is erased at runtime. Workaround: pass `Class<T>` and use reflection.
`new HashMap<>()` — infers type from the left. Java 7+. Cuts boilerplate.

quiz.explain: Upper-bounded wildcard is a producer — you read, you can't add.

quiz.q: Generics in Java are implemented via…
quiz.explain: Erasure preserves backwards compat with pre-Java 5 code.

quiz.q: Why can't you write `new T()` inside a generic class?
options: T is erased at runtime — the JVM doesn't know what T is
quiz.explain: No runtime type info for T.

quiz.q: For a copy method: `copy(List<? extends T> src, List<? super T> dst)`. Which is the PRODUCER?
quiz.explain: src produces values you read out; dst consumes values you add.
Wildcard is bounded — you can't accidentally add wrong types.

challenges.text: Write a generic method `<T extends Comparable<T>> T max(List<T> list)` returning the biggest element.
Convert a raw `List` in existing code to `List<String>`. Fix every compile warning.
Implement generic `Stack<T>` with push/pop/peek/isEmpty backed by ArrayList.
Write `copy(List<? extends T> from, List<? super T> to)`. First without wildcards; see the errors.
Build a type-safe heterogeneous container: put/get keyed by `Class<T>` returning the right type.
Write a generic `Pair<A, B>` with equals/hashCode. Verify it works as a HashMap key.
Design a generic Event Bus: `subscribe(Class<E>, Consumer<E>)` and `publish(E)`. Handle erasure via Class<T> tokens.

interviewQuestions: What problem do generics solve, and what did we do before them?
What is type erasure, and what are its consequences?
Can you create a generic array in Java? Why or why not?
What's the difference between `List<Object>`, `List<?>`, and raw `List`?
What is a bounded type parameter?
Why is `List<Dog>` not a subtype of `List<Animal>` even if Dog extends Animal?
